ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for the specific Zi Char preparation 'Garlic Butter Lala'. Closest defensible anchor is the generic clam food product term. Dish-level specificity (garlic butter sauce, stir-fried preparation) is not captured in FoodOn as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Garlic Butter Lala as served at a Zi Char stall typically weighs approximately 400 g, comprising whole clams in shell with sauce, shared among 2–3 diners as part of a multi-dish meal.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Clams, mixed species, cooked, moist heat (FDC ID 175159) combined with butter and garlic component estimates from USDA SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Garlic Butter Lala exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA FDC cooked clam data (FDC ID 175159) with USDA SR Legacy butter and garlic component contributions, weighted to reflect a typical Zi Char preparation ratio of approximately 60% clam meat, 15% butter, 10% garlic/chilli/sauce, 15% cooking liquid. Input per_100g values (energy 120 kcal, protein 8.75 g, fat 7.5 g, carbohydrate 4.5 g) are broadly consistent with this component-weighted estimate and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium at 450 mg/100g reflects sauce-heavy Zi Char preparation and is plausible given oyster sauce and soy sauce contributions.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — analogous estimate based on shellfish/seafood with minimal carbohydrate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Garlic Butter Lala. GI estimated at approximately 15 based on the University of Sydney GI Database analogous category for shellfish and low-carbohydrate seafood dishes. Clams have negligible starch content; the primary carbohydrate contribution (4.5 g/100g) comes from oyster sauce and minor starch thickeners, which have a moderate-to-high GI individually but contribute minimally to overall glycaemic load. Glycaemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 18 g) / 100 = 2.7, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 4.5 × 4 = 18 g. This is a very low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 450 mg per 100 g from oyster sauce and soy sauce; a 400 g plate delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium, representing 78–90% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000–2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce preparation, limit portion to a small shared serve, and avoid pairing with other high-sodium dishes in the same meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content at 4.5 g per 100 g and very low glycaemic load per serving (GL ≈ 2.7) make this dish minimally impactful on postprandial blood glucose.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes can include Garlic Butter Lala as a protein-rich, low-GI dish; however, they should request minimal oyster sauce to reduce added sugar and avoid pairing with large portions of white rice to keep the overall meal glycaemic load controlled.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 3.75 g per 100 g from butter and trans fat at 0.13 g per 100 g are relevant; a 400 g plate delivers approximately 15 g saturated fat and 0.52 g trans fat, alongside high sodium (1800 mg) which elevates cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should request less butter or a substitution with a plant-based oil, ask for reduced-sauce preparation to lower sodium, and limit portion size to reduce saturated fat and trans fat intake per meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Clams provide anti-inflammatory zinc and selenium; however, high sodium (450 mg/100g) may worsen tissue oedema, and butter contributes saturated fat which can promote systemic inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the anti-inflammatory shellfish protein but should request reduced butter and sauce to lower saturated fat and sodium; those with nightshade sensitivity should note that chilli padi is a nightshade and may aggravate symptoms.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Garlic Butter Lala is a popular Singaporean and Malaysian Zi Char seafood dish consisting of fresh lala clams (Anadara granosa or similar small bivalves) stir-fried at high heat in a rich sauce of butter, minced garlic, chilli padi, oyster sauce, and light soy sauce, often finished with spring onion. The dish is characterised by its intensely savoury, umami-rich sauce with a pronounced garlic aroma and a spicy kick from the chilli. The clam shells open during cooking, releasing their natural brine into the sauce and creating a deeply flavourful broth. Texturally, the clam meat is tender and slightly chewy, while the sauce is glossy and buttery. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 120 kcal, 8.75 g protein, 7.5 g fat (3.75 g saturated), and 4.5 g carbohydrate, with notably high sodium at 450 mg from the sauce components. The dish is a good source of shellfish protein and provides iron (1.5 mg) and calcium (25 mg) from the clams. Clinically, the high sodium content is the primary concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients; the low carbohydrate and very low glycaemic load make it suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management when paired with reduced sauce. Garlic Butter Lala is typically ordered as a shared dish at Zi Char restaurants and hawker centres across Singapore and Malaysia, served alongside steamed white rice and other stir-fried dishes. It is not halal due to the shellfish content and is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Garlic Butter Lala as a composite dish exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FDC cooked clam data combined with butter and sauce component contributions. The input values from the ai_generated grabfood record are broadly consistent with this component-weighted estimate and are retained. Medium confidence reflects the absence of a direct approved-source match for this specific dish preparation.

Dish is not halal due to shellfish content. Primary cuisine classification is Singaporean/Malaysian Zi Char; input listed 'Malaysian' as primary cuisine but the dish is equally associated with Singapore Zi Char culture — both are noted. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate for low-carbohydrate shellfish dishes; no direct GI measurement for Garlic Butter Lala was found in the University of Sydney GI Database.
